%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# B.5-3 Repeated Factors of Q(x)

> **Section Objective:** Learn how partial fractions change when Q(x) contains a repeated linear factor.

## Concepts In This Section

- Repeated denominator factor
- Repeated-factor partial-fraction template
- Derivative Heaviside coefficient formula
- Example B.10 expansion

## 1. Recognize the Repeated Factor

Here \((x-\lambda)^r\) is the **repeated factor**: the same linear factor \((x-\lambda)\) appears \(r\) times in the denominator. The remaining terms \((x-\alpha_1),(x-\alpha_2),\ldots,(x-\alpha_j)\) are ordinary **distinct** linear factors, each appearing only once.

**Symbol guide:** \(P(x)\) is the numerator polynomial; \(\lambda\) is the root of the repeated factor; \(r\) is the repetition order; \(\alpha_i\) are the roots of the distinct factors.

**Minimal example:** For \((x+1)^3(x+2)\), the repeated factor is \((x+1)^3\), so \(\lambda=-1\) and \(r=3\). The factor \((x+2)\) is distinct.

> **Exam trigger:** Any denominator factor with exponent greater than 1 signals a repeated factor.

#### COMMON MISUSE
Do not treat \((x+1)^3\) as if it creates only one partial-fraction term. It creates three.

$$F(x)=\frac{P(x)}{(x-\lambda)^r(x-\alpha_1)(x-\alpha_2)\cdots(x-\alpha_j)}$$

## 2. Build the Repeated-Factor Template

A repeated factor of order \(r\) produces **exactly \(r\) partial-fraction terms** — one for each denominator power from \(1\) through \(r\). You cannot skip any power.

- \(A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_r\) are the coefficients for the repeated factor \((x-\lambda)\).
- \(B_1, \ldots, B_j\) are the coefficients for the remaining distinct factors.

**Concrete template for \((x+1)^3(x+2)\):**

$$\frac{A_1}{x+1}+\frac{A_2}{(x+1)^2}+\frac{A_3}{(x+1)^3}+\frac{B_1}{x+2}$$

All four terms are required. The denominator \((x+1)^3\) contributes three terms (powers 1, 2, and 3), and \((x+2)\) contributes one.

> **Exam note:** Always write the full template before solving for any coefficient.

#### COMMON TRAP
Skipping the lower-power terms \(A_1/(x+1)\) and \(A_2/(x+1)^2\) and writing only \(A_3/(x+1)^3\) is the most frequent repeated-factor error.

$$F(x)=\frac{A_1}{x-\lambda}+\frac{A_2}{(x-\lambda)^2}+\cdots+\frac{A_r}{(x-\lambda)^r}+\frac{B_1}{x-\alpha_1}+\cdots+\frac{B_j}{x-\alpha_j}$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 3. Find the Repeated-Factor Coefficients

This is the **derivative form of the Heaviside cover-up method**, extended to handle repeated factors. The index \(k\) runs from \(0, 1, \ldots, r-1\).

**Symbol guide:**
- \(A_{r-k}\) — the coefficient being solved for
- \(k\) — the order of the derivative taken
- \(r\) — the order of the repeated factor
- \(\lambda\) — the root of the repeated factor (i.e., the value where \(x-\lambda=0\))

**How to use it:**

| \(k\) | Derivative order | Coefficient found |
|--------|-----------------|-------------------|
| \(0\) | none (evaluate directly) | \(A_r\) (highest power) |
| \(1\) | first derivative | \(A_{r-1}\) |
| \(2\) | second derivative | \(A_{r-2}\) |

So \(k=0\) gives the **highest-power** coefficient \(A_r\) by simple substitution, while each successive derivative peels off the next lower coefficient.

> **Exam trigger:** Use this formula whenever a repeated factor remains after ordinary cover-up.

#### COMMON MISUSE
Do not apply ordinary cover-up separately to each of \(A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_r\). Only \(A_r\) can be found by direct substitution; the rest require derivatives.

$$A_{r-k}=\frac{1}{k!}\left.\frac{d^k}{dx^k}\left[(x-\lambda)^rF(x)\right]\right|_{x=\lambda}$$

## 4. Representative Example: Example B.10

**Original function:**

$$F(x)=\frac{4x^3+16x^2+23x+13}{(x+1)^3(x+2)}$$

**Step 1 — Write the template first.** Because \((x+1)^3\) is a repeated factor of order 3, the template must be:

$$\frac{A_1}{x+1}+\frac{A_2}{(x+1)^2}+\frac{A_3}{(x+1)^3}+\frac{B_1}{x+2}$$

**Step 2 — Find the coefficients.** Applying the derivative formula:
- \(A_3 = 2\) — found by evaluating \((x+1)^3 F(x)\) at \(x=-1\) with no derivative (\(k=0\)).
- \(A_2 = 1\) — found using the first derivative (\(k=1\)).
- \(A_1 = 3\) — found using the second derivative (\(k=2\)).
- \(B_1 = 1\) — found by ordinary cover-up at \(x=-2\).

**Final expansion** (shown above as the formula for this block).

> **Exam note:** Writing the correct template first makes coefficient solving systematic and error-free. Never skip the template step.

$$F(x)=\frac{3}{x+1}+\frac{1}{(x+1)^2}+\frac{2}{(x+1)^3}+\frac{1}{x+2}$$

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Repeated factor \((x-\lambda)^r\) in \(F(x)=P(x)/[(x-\lambda)^r(x-\alpha_1)\cdots(x-\alpha_j)]\) signals a multi-term expansion.
- Template rule: one term per power, \(A_1/(x-\lambda)+A_2/(x-\lambda)^2+\cdots+A_r/(x-\lambda)^r\); never skip a power.
- Coefficient formula: \(A_{r-k}=\dfrac{1}{k!}\left.\dfrac{d^k}{dx^k}[(x-\lambda)^rF(x)]\right|_{x=\lambda}\), for \(k=0,1,\ldots,r-1\).
- Example B.10 result: \(F(x)=\dfrac{3}{x+1}+\dfrac{1}{(x+1)^2}+\dfrac{2}{(x+1)^3}+\dfrac{1}{x+2}\).

*Next, use this repeated-factor skill whenever inverse transforms or differential-equation solutions produce repeated roots.*
